title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
authors: Chelsey J.F. Smith, Paul J. Hoover,

items.subtopic: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus | DEFINITION, PREVALENCE, AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Prevalence and Demographics of SLE
items.content: SLE is more prevalent in women, particularly those of childbearing age, with a significant racial and ethnic disparity. The CDC estimates 204,295 cases in the U.S., with prevalence nine times higher in women than men.
keyPoints: 90% of SLE patients are women, mostly of childbearing age.
CDC estimates prevalence at 204,295 cases in the U.S.
Prevalence is nine times higher in women compared to men.
Highest prevalence among Black women, followed by Hispanic, White, and Asian/Pacific Islander women.

items.subtopic: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus | DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for SLE: EULAR/ACR and SLICC
items.content: The diagnosis of SLE requires an individualized approach using the 2019 EULAR/ACR or 2012 SLICC criteria, with ANA titer ≥1:80 as a prerequisite. Patients need at least 10 points across clinical domains to meet criteria.
keyPoints: EULAR/ACR and SLICC are used for SLE classification in trials.
ANA titer of at least 1:80 is required.
At least 10 points needed from clinical domains for diagnosis.
Renal biopsy revealing class III or IV lupus nephritis can alone meet criteria.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · Systemic Lupus Erythematosus | DEFINITION, PREVALENCE, AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus | DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Categorization of SLE: Type 1 vs. Type 2
items.content: SLE is categorized into type 1, with classic immune dysregulation symptoms like nephritis and arthritis, and type 2, characterized by fatigue and cognitive dysfunction. Type 1 responds to immunosuppression, while type 2 does not.
keyPoints: Type 1 lupus involves classic findings like nephritis and arthritis.
Type 2 lupus includes symptoms such as fatigue and cognitive dysfunction.
Type 1 is responsive to standard immunosuppressive therapy.
Type 2 patients are less responsive to immunosuppression.

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW | GENETICS | ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Genetic and Environmental Interplay in SLE Pathogenesis
items.content: SLE development is influenced by a combination of genetic predispositions and environmental factors. Genetic variations, especially those affecting immunoregulatory genes, contribute significantly to the disease's pathogenesis. Environmental triggers such as viral infections, UV light exposure, and toxins further modulate disease expression.
keyPoints: Genetic component evidenced by familial clustering and higher incidence in monozygotic twins (24%) compared to dizygotic twins (2%).
Over 150 susceptibility loci identified from GWAS, with many linked to type 1 interferon production or signaling pathways.
Environmental factors like viral infections (e.g., Epstein-Barr virus), UV light exposure, and low socioeconomic status contribute to SLE development by interacting with genetic predispositions.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · OVERVIEW | GENETICS | ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW | GENETICS | ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Role of Interferon Pathways in SLE Susceptibility
items.content: Interferon pathways play a critical role in the susceptibility and pathogenesis of SLE. Genetic variations affecting nucleic acid sensing, metabolism, degradation, and IFN response amplification are key contributors to disease development.
keyPoints: Nearly half of SLE susceptibility loci are associated with type 1 interferon production or signaling.
Genes like TLR7, ADAR, IFIH1, SAMHD1, RNASEH2B, TREX1 affect nucleic acid sensing and metabolism, increasing IFN levels.
DNASE1, DNASE1L3 are involved in nucleic acid degradation, while IRF5, IRF7, IRF8, STAT4 amplify the IFN response.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · OVERVIEW | GENETICS | ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: INNATE IMMUNITY | ADAPTIVE IMMUNITY
items.title: Role of Neutrophils and Macrophages in SLE Pathogenesis
items.content: In Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE), neutrophils have increased turnover, leading to an excess of cellular debris. This overwhelms the clearance capacity of macrophages and dendritic cells, which are dysregulated in their ability to clear immune complexes and nucleic acids through complement-mediated phagocytosis.
keyPoints: Neutrophils in SLE have higher turnover, contributing to increased cellular debris.
Macrophage and dendritic cell dysfunction impairs clearance of cellular debris and immune complexes.
Excess cellular debris triggers type 1 interferon (IFN) production via nucleic acid sensors and toll-like receptors (TLRs).
Type 1 IFN further primes neutrophils for turnover, exacerbating the cycle.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · INNATE IMMUNITY | ADAPTIVE IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INNATE IMMUNITY | ADAPTIVE IMMUNITY
items.title: B Cell Dysfunction in SLE Pathogenesis
items.content: In SLE, B cells lose tolerance and produce autoantibodies that contribute to tissue inflammation. Age-associated B cells (ABCs) expand and mature into plasma cells secreting pathogenic autoantibodies, while T regulatory cells fail to maintain tolerance.
keyPoints: B cells in SLE lose tolerance, leading to the production of autoantibodies.
Autoantibodies complexed with nucleic acids are key mediators of tissue inflammation and damage.
Age-associated B cells (ABCs) expand and mature into pathogenic plasma cells in SLE patients.
T regulatory cells are defective, failing to maintain tolerance in T and B cells.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · INNATE IMMUNITY | ADAPTIVE IMMUNITY

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Understanding SLE Presentation and Treatment Goals
items.content: SLE can present with a wide range of symptoms from mild to severe, often requiring ongoing therapy adjustments. The treatment goal is achieving low-level disease activity or remission.
keyPoints: Initial presentation of SLE is variable, ranging from nonspecific constitutional symptoms to multiorgan involvement.
Approximately 15% of patients have relatively mild disease, which may include fatigue, brain fog, and/or mild arthralgia.
Goals for SLE treatment are low-level disease activity or remission on ongoing therapy.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Recognizing Musculoskeletal Manifestations in SLE
items.content: SLE patients often experience nonspecific arthralgia and myalgia, with lupus arthritis affecting specific joints. Imaging studies suggest possible erosive patterns.
keyPoints: Nonspecific arthralgia and myalgia are common in SLE.
Lupus arthritis commonly involves the wrists, metacarpal-phalangeal, proximal interphalangeal joints of the hands, and knees.
Pain out of proportion to other joints may indicate avascular necrosis, especially with corticosteroid use.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Identifying Cutaneous Manifestations of SLE
items.content: SLE presents with various skin manifestations, including ACLE, SCLE, and chronic forms. The malar rash is a classic sign of ACLE.
keyPoints: ACLE occurs with SLE disease activity; the malar rash appears in a 'butterfly' distribution across the cheeks.
SCLE is associated with anti-Ro or SSA antibodies and presents as flat, red-rimmed annular lesions.
Approximately one-third of SLE patients experience Raynaud's phenomenon.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · OVERVIEW AND SYSTEMIC MANIFESTATIONS | MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS | CUTANEOUS MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: RENAL MANIFESTATIONS | NERVOUS SYSTEM MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Screening and Management of Lupus Nephritis
items.content: Lupus nephritis occurs in approximately 35% of SLE patients, with non-White patients experiencing more severe outcomes. Regular screening every three months using UPCr or a 24-hour urine test is recommended to detect asymptomatic cases. Elevated anti-dsDNA and decreased C3/C4 levels are warning signs for active nephritis. Renal biopsy is indicated when UPCr exceeds 0.5 or kidney function declines, with aggressive treatment reserved for classes III, IV, or combinations including class V. Class V disease has a better prognosis but requires similar management to more severe classes.
keyPoints: Nephritis occurs in ~35% of SLE patients
Non-White patients have more severe outcomes
Screening every 3 months with UPCr or 24-hour urine test
Elevated anti-dsDNA and decreased C3/C4 as warning signs
Renal biopsy indicated for UPCr >0.5 or declining kidney function
Aggressive therapy for class III, IV, or combinations including V
~20% of SLE patients with class IV die or develop ESRD within 10 years
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · RENAL MANIFESTATIONS | NERVOUS SYSTEM MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: RENAL MANIFESTATIONS | NERVOUS SYSTEM MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Neuropsychiatric Manifestations in Lupus Patients
items.content: Lupus patients may experience neuropsychiatric symptoms ranging from diffuse 'brain fog' to focal deficits like seizures. The etiology of cognitive dysfunction is unclear but may involve autoantibodies crossing the blood-brain barrier. Headaches, often severe, can mimic migraines or tension headaches. Acute psychosis and confusional states are rare without structural abnormalities, necessitating exclusion of glucocorticoid-induced psychosis and infectious encephalitis. Transverse myelitis, a rare complication, presents with bilateral motor weakness and sensory deficits due to spinal cord inflammation.
keyPoints: Neuropsychiatric symptoms include 'brain fog' and seizures
'Brain fog' may be related to autoantibodies crossing the blood-brain barrier
Headaches can mimic migraines or tension headaches
Acute psychosis and confusional states are rare without structural abnormalities
Transverse myelitis presents with bilateral motor weakness and sensory deficits

items.subtopic: VASCULAR OCCLUSION: MYOCARDIAL INFARCTIONS AND STROKE | PULMONARY MANIFESTATIONS | CARDIAC MANIFESTATIONS | HEMATOLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Increased Risk of Vascular Thrombosis in SLE with APS
items.content: Patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) have an elevated risk for coronary artery disease and vascular thrombosis, particularly when complicated by antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). The presence of antiphospholipid antibodies contributes to prothrombotic properties, increasing the likelihood of arterial, venous, or small vessel thromboembolic events. Stroke is notably prevalent in up to 19% of SLE patients due to atherosclerosis and APS.
keyPoints: Coronary heart disease from accelerated atherosclerosis is significant in young SLE patients without other risk factors.
Vascular injury from excessive type 1 IFN predisposes to atherosclerosis.
Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) increases the risk of vascular thrombosis and stroke, occurring in up to 19% of SLE cases.
APS is associated with central nervous system manifestations including seizures and transverse myelitis.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · VASCULAR OCCLUSION: MYOCARDIAL INFARCTIONS AND STROKE | PULMONARY MANIFESTATIONS | CARDIAC MANIFESTATIONS | HEMATOLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: VASCULAR OCCLUSION: MYOCARDIAL INFARCTIONS AND STROKE | PULMONARY MANIFESTATIONS | CARDIAC MANIFESTATIONS | HEMATOLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Hematologic Manifestations of SLE and Associated Risks
items.content: SLE frequently presents with cytopenias, including anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia. Anemia is the most common hematologic manifestation, occurring in over 50% of cases, often due to chronic disease or autoimmune processes. Leukopenia, particularly lymphopenia, occurs in about half of SLE patients. Thrombocytopenia can range from mild to severe and may be linked with APS, posing a significant bleeding risk.
keyPoints: Anemia is present in >50% of SLE cases; one-third due to anemia of chronic disease.
Leukopenia (<4000/μL) occurs in about half of SLE patients, usually as lymphopenia (<1500/μL).
Thrombocytopenia (<150,000/μL), often immune-mediated, can be severe (<50,000/μL) and linked with APS.
Treatment options for cytopenias include glucocorticoids, anti-CD20 agents, platelet growth factors, IV immunoglobulin, or splenectomy.

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS | OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS | AUTOANTIBODIES
items.title: Management of Mesenteric Vasculitis in SLE
items.content: Mesenteric vasculitis, a serious complication of SLE, can lead to intestinal perforation, bleeding, and ischemia. It necessitates aggressive treatment with high doses of glucocorticoids.
keyPoints: Mesenteric vasculitis requires high doses of glucocorticoids for treatment.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GASTROINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS | OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS | AUTOANTIBODIES

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS | OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS | AUTOANTIBODIES
items.title: Importance of ANA Testing in SLE Diagnosis
items.content: ANA testing is crucial for diagnosing SLE, with almost all patients exhibiting positive results. The EULAR/ACR criteria require an ANA titer of 1:80 for clinical trials, underscoring its diagnostic importance.
keyPoints: ANA presence and titer are the best screening tests for SLE.
Almost all SLE patients have positive ANAs.
EULAR/ACR criteria require an ANA titer of 1:80 for enrollment in clinical trials.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GASTROINTESTINAL MANIFESTATIONS | OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS | AUTOANTIBODIES

items.subtopic: STANDARD DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | ■ TESTS FOR FOLLOWING DISEASE COURSE
items.title: Importance of ANA and Anti-dsDNA in SLE Diagnosis
items.content: ANA titers are crucial for diagnosing SLE, with a threshold of 1:80 or higher indicating high likelihood. In cases where ANAs are negative but clinical suspicion is high, anti-Ro (SSA) can be indicative. Anti-dsDNA antibodies and low complement levels (C3, C4) suggest lupus nephritis.
keyPoints: ANA positive at 1:80 or higher favors SLE diagnosis
Anti-Ro (SSA) may indicate SLE in ANA-negative cases
Anti-dsDNA antibodies and low C3/C4 levels suggest lupus nephritis
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · STANDARD DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | ■ TESTS FOR FOLLOWING DISEASE COURSE

items.subtopic: STANDARD DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | ■ TESTS FOR FOLLOWING DISEASE COURSE
items.title: Monitoring Disease Activity in SLE with Anti-dsDNA and Complement Levels
items.content: Anti-dsDNA antibody titers are key for tracking SLE disease activity due to their rapid turnover. Complement levels (C3, C4) drop during high disease activity episodes, reflecting immune complex formation.
keyPoints: Track anti-dsDNA antibodies for disease activity
Complement levels (C3, C4) decrease with high disease activity
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · STANDARD DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | ■ TESTS FOR FOLLOWING DISEASE COURSE

items.subtopic: STANDARD DIAGNOSTIC TESTS | ■ TESTS FOR FOLLOWING DISEASE COURSE
items.title: EULAR Recommendations for SLE and Lupus Nephritis Management
items.content: EULAR guidelines emphasize achieving low disease activity or remission, defined by a SLEDAI score of 0–4 or 0. Treatment aims to minimize symptoms with minimal corticosteroid use, reserving aggressive therapy for life-threatening manifestations.
keyPoints: SLE treatment goals: low disease activity (SLEDAI 0-4) or remission (SLEDAI 0)
Minimize corticosteroids; reserve aggressive therapy for severe cases

items.subtopic: ESSENTIAL LUPUS THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES AND ANTIMALARIALS
items.title: Why Use Antimalarials in SLE?
items.content: Antimalarials are recommended for all SLE patients unless contraindicated due to their ability to prevent flares, increase survival, and decrease risks of renal disease and accelerated atherosclerosis. HCQ is the most common antimalarial used as first-line therapy, especially beneficial for skin manifestations, arthritis, and fatigue.
keyPoints: Antimalarials should be used in all SLE patients unless contraindicated.
Benefits include preventing flares, increasing survival, reducing renal disease risk, and decreasing accelerated atherosclerosis.
HCQ is first-line therapy for skin manifestations and arthritis; improves fatigue.
Use during pregnancy and breastfeeding has been shown to be safe.
Dosing up to 5 mg/kg per day with retinal evaluations at baseline and yearly intervals.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · ESSENTIAL LUPUS THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES AND ANTIMALARIALS

items.subtopic: ESSENTIAL LUPUS THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES AND ANTIMALARIALS
items.title: HCQ Dosing and Monitoring in SLE Treatment
items.content: HCQ dosing is under investigation, with current recommendations up to 5 mg/kg per day based on actual body weight. Regular retinal evaluations are necessary due to the risk of macular effects from prolonged use. Higher doses may offer better disease control but increase the risk of retinal toxicity.
keyPoints: HCQ dosing recommendation: up to 5 mg/kg per day.
Retinal evaluations at baseline and yearly intervals for monitoring.
Risk factors for retinal toxicity include higher dose and longer duration.
Higher doses may confer better disease control but increase maculopathy risk.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · ESSENTIAL LUPUS THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES AND ANTIMALARIALS

items.subtopic: GLUCOCORTICOIDS | SKIN AND MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS: IMMUNOMODULATING AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT DRUGS
items.title: Optimizing Glucocorticoid Use in SLE Management
items.content: Glucocorticoids are pivotal for managing severe manifestations of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), particularly during acute exacerbations. However, their use is associated with significant side effects that necessitate careful dosing and prompt tapering.
keyPoints: Dosing should be based on organ-specific needs and disease severity.
Side effects include cushingoid metabolic effects, hyperglycemia, hypertension, osteoporosis, and avascular necrosis.
Taper to maintenance doses of 5 mg or less of daily prednisone or equivalent as soon as feasible.

Intravenous methylprednisolone (125–1000 mg daily for 1–3 days) is reserved for emergent situations like active nephritis, severe CNS or vascular involvement, and life-threatening hematologic manifestations.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | SKIN AND MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS: IMMUNOMODULATING AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT DRUGS

items.subtopic: GLUCOCORTICOIDS | SKIN AND MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS: IMMUNOMODULATING AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT DRUGS
items.title: Role of Immunomodulating and Immunosuppressant Drugs in SLE Skin and Musculoskeletal Manifestations
items.content: For patients with persistent mucocutaneous disease or arthritis, additional immunomodulating drugs can be considered to manage symptoms and reduce reliance on glucocorticoids.
keyPoints: Topical sunscreens and antimalarials are first-line for skin manifestations.
Options include topical glucocorticoids and calcineurin inhibitors if unresponsive.
Oral immunomodulating drugs like azathioprine, leflunomide, mycophenolate mofetil (MMF), or mycophenolic acid can be considered for persistent disease.
FDA-approved biologicals such as belimumab and anifrolumab improve outcomes and aid in glucocorticoid tapering.
Thalidomide and lenalidomide are used with caution due to risks of polyneuropathy and teratogenicity.
Methotrexate is an option for SLE-associated arthritis.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | SKIN AND MUSCULOSKELETAL MANIFESTATIONS: IMMUNOMODULATING AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT DRUGS

items.subtopic: RENAL DISEASE: SUCCESSES WITH COMBINATION THERAPIES
items.title: Targeted Therapeutic Goals in Lupus Nephritis
items.content: Treatment targets for lupus nephritis include specific reductions in urine protein-to-creatinine (UPCr) levels over time, with associated goals for estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). These targets are crucial for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic efficacy.
keyPoints: 3-month target: ≥25% reduction in UPCr
6-month target: ≥50% reduction in UPCr to <3 g/d
12-24 month target: UPCr to <0.5–0.7 g/d, with eGFR within 10% of baseline
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · RENAL DISEASE: SUCCESSES WITH COMBINATION THERAPIES

items.subtopic: RENAL DISEASE: SUCCESSES WITH COMBINATION THERAPIES
items.title: Induction Therapy Considerations in Lupus Nephritis
items.content: Initial treatment regimens for lupus nephritis include cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) with glucocorticoids. Ethnicity influences the choice of induction therapy, and alternative dosing strategies may improve tolerability while maintaining efficacy.
keyPoints: Cyclophosphamide or MMF plus glucocorticoids for induction
MMF 2–3 g daily recommended for Black/Hispanic patients
Low-dose cyclophosphamide (500 mg every 2 weeks) followed by azathioprine or MMF maintenance is effective
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · RENAL DISEASE: SUCCESSES WITH COMBINATION THERAPIES

items.subtopic: RENAL DISEASE: SUCCESSES WITH COMBINATION THERAPIES
items.title: Role of Immunomodulating Therapies in Lupus Nephritis
items.content: FDA-approved immunomodulating therapies, such as belimumab and voclosporin, offer alternative treatment options for lupus nephritis. These can be used alongside standard therapies to potentially reduce the need for high-dose steroids.
keyPoints: Belimumab approved in 2020
Voclosporin approved in 2021
Voclosporin-based or belimumab-based triple therapy as initial treatment options

items.subtopic: THERAPIES FOR OTHER ORGAN-BASED SYSTEMS | EMERGING THERAPIES AND TREATMENT APPROACHES
items.title: Use of Anti-CD20 Agents in Severe SLE Manifestations
items.content: Anti-CD20 agents such as rituximab are utilized for treating lupus-related hemolytic anemia and severe thrombocytopenia. They also form a core part of the treatment regimen for serious central and peripheral inflammatory nervous system presentations, including psychosis, vasculitis, and transverse myelitis.
keyPoints: Rituximab is used for lupus-related hemolytic anemia and severe thrombocytopenia.
Treatment includes MMF, cyclophosphamide, rituximab plus corticosteroids for serious central/peripheral nervous system inflammation.
Plasmapheresis and high levels of glucocorticoids are lifesaving in atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
Rituximab and eculizumab (C5 inhibitor) used in refractory cases.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · THERAPIES FOR OTHER ORGAN-BASED SYSTEMS | EMERGING THERAPIES AND TREATMENT APPROACHES

items.subtopic: EMERGING THERAPIES AND TREATMENT APPROACHES
items.title: Emerging Therapies for SLE: Focus on Lupus Nephritis and Beyond
items.content: SGLT2 inhibitors are being explored for lupus nephritis due to their potential in preserving renal function through tubular protection. Obinutuzumab, a CD20-targeted monoclonal antibody, has shown efficacy in phase 3 studies for lupus nephritis and is under investigation for non-nephritic SLE. Deucravacitinib (Tyk2 inhibitor) and telitacicept are in phase 3 trials, while litifilimab targets BDCA2 to suppress type I interferons.
keyPoints: SGLT2 inhibitors studied for tubular protection in lupus nephritis.
Obinutuzumab shows efficacy in lupus nephritis and is being investigated for SLE without nephritis.
Deucravacitinib, a Tyk2 inhibitor, is in phase 3 study for SLE.
Telitacicept approved in China; global studies underway.
Litifilimab targets BDCA2 to suppress type I interferons.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · THERAPIES FOR OTHER ORGAN-BASED SYSTEMS | EMERGING THERAPIES AND TREATMENT APPROACHES

items.subtopic: GUIDELINES | DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS
items.title: Balancing Immunosuppression Duration in Lupus Nephritis
items.content: The KDIGO guidelines emphasize the importance of balancing the duration of immunosuppression with the risk of lupus relapse and adverse effects. For patients achieving complete renal response without extrarenal manifestations, a minimum total duration of 36 months is suggested.
keyPoints: KDIGO guidelines recommend not less than 36 months for total immunosuppression in specific cases
Balance between risk of lupus relapse and adverse effects from immunosuppressive drugs
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GUIDELINES | DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS

items.subtopic: GUIDELINES | DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS
items.title: Diagnosing Drug-Induced Lupus (DIL)
items.content: DIL is characterized by a positive ANA and clinical features of SLE due to drug exposure, with antihistone antibodies being common. Diagnosis involves distinguishing DIL from SLE through laboratory tests, as symptoms may start weeks to months after drug initiation.
keyPoints: DIL accounts for ~6–12% of all lupus cases
Antihistone antibodies are positive in 75% of DIL cases
Procainamide has a high incidence risk of up to 30%
Symptoms resolve after discontinuation of the offending drug
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · GUIDELINES | DRUG-INDUCED LUPUS

items.subtopic: FERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION/HORMONE REPLACEMENT | PREGNANCY
items.title: Contraception and Hormone Replacement Considerations in SLE Patients
items.content: Women with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) require careful consideration regarding contraception and hormone replacement due to increased risks associated with their condition. Estrogen compounds should be avoided, especially in patients with antiphospholipid antibodies or active disease, because of the heightened risk for thromboembolism.
keyPoints: Estrogen compounds increase thromboembolism risk in SLE patients with antiphospholipid antibodies or moderate-to-severe disease.
Progestin-only options like levonorgestrel intrauterine device and etonogestrel implant are safe for contraception in SLE.
Postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy should avoid estrogen if antiphospholipid antibodies are present.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · FERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION/HORMONE REPLACEMENT | PREGNANCY

items.subtopic: FERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION/HORMONE REPLACEMENT | PREGNANCY
items.title: Management of Pregnancy in SLE Patients
items.content: Pregnancies in women with SLE are considered high risk due to potential disease flares and adverse outcomes. Active disease at conception is linked to more frequent flares, while hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) use reduces flare risks during pregnancy.
keyPoints: SLE pregnancies have a higher risk of adverse outcomes such as preeclampsia, preterm birth, and maternal death.
Active disease at conception is associated with increased flares during pregnancy.
HCQ is recommended to reduce the risk of flares and is safe for all SLE pregnancies.
Low-dose aspirin is used to prevent preeclampsia in pregnant women with SLE.
Azathioprine and tacrolimus are considered safe for managing severe disease or renal involvement during pregnancy.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · FERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION/HORMONE REPLACEMENT | PREGNANCY

items.subtopic: NEONATAL LUPUS | OUTCOMES | TREAT-TO-TARGET APPROACHES
items.title: Management of Neonatal Lupus in the Presence of Maternal Ro Antibodies
items.content: Neonatal lupus is a risk for infants born to mothers with high titers of SS-A (Ro) antibodies. These antibodies can cross the placenta, leading to conditions like transient cutaneous rash, lab abnormalities, or congenital heart block. High-risk obstetricians should be involved early, particularly if fetal echocardiography indicates first- or second-degree heart block, where fluorinated steroids may be considered. Additionally, hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) use during pregnancy can significantly reduce the risk of congenital heart block in subsequent pregnancies.
keyPoints: SS-A (Ro) antibodies cross the placenta and increase neonatal lupus risk.
Congenital heart block is a serious manifestation that may present in utero.
Fetal echocardiography should be performed for high-risk cases.
Fluorinated steroids are considered if first- or second-degree heart block is detected.
HCQ use during pregnancy reduces the risk of congenital heart block by 90%.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · NEONATAL LUPUS | OUTCOMES | TREAT-TO-TARGET APPROACHES

items.subtopic: NEONATAL LUPUS | OUTCOMES | TREAT-TO-TARGET APPROACHES
items.title: Treat-to-Target Approaches in SLE: Achieving Low Disease Activity and Remission
items.content: In systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), treat-to-target strategies such as LLDAS (Low Lupus Disease Activity State) and DORIS (DORis remission criteria for SLE) are used to guide treatment goals. These approaches utilize the SLEDAI-2K instrument to measure disease activity, aiming for low or no disease activity with minimal steroid use and stable immunosuppressant therapy. Achieving LLDAS is possible in up to 80% of patients, and maintaining it over two years correlates with significantly reduced overall damage.
keyPoints: LLDAS and DORIS are validated treat-to-target goals for SLE management.
SLEDAI-2K is used to measure disease activity for these targets.
LLDAS involves low disease activity, minimal steroid use, and stable immunosuppressant therapy.
Up to 80% of patients can achieve LLDAS.
Maintaining LLDAS over two years significantly reduces overall damage.
items.reference: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus · NEONATAL LUPUS | OUTCOMES | TREAT-TO-TARGET APPROACHES

items.subtopic: DAMAGE
items.title: Glucocorticoid-Induced Damage in SLE
items.content: In Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE), glucocorticoids, while essential for managing disease activity, contribute to irreversible organ damage independent of disease activity. Complications such as cataracts, osteoporosis-associated fractures, avascular necrosis, and diabetes mellitus are common due to glucocorticoid use.
keyPoints: Use of glucocorticoids leads to irreversible organ damage in SLE.
Common complications include cataracts, osteoporosis-associated fractures, avascular necrosis, and diabetes mellitus.
Damage accrues even at low doses (e.g., 5 mg prednisone or equivalent).

items.subtopic: DAMAGE
items.title: Cardiovascular Risk and Malignancy in SLE
items.content: SLE patients face an increased risk of accelerated atherosclerosis, leading to higher mortality rates from myocardial infarction and cerebrovascular events after 5 years. This is due to traditional cardiovascular risk factors as well as non-traditional factors like antiphospholipid antibodies, low C3 levels, high glucocorticoid doses, and elevated homocysteine and leptin levels. Additionally, SLE patients have an increased overall cancer risk and cancer-related death, with specific site-specific cancers identified.
keyPoints: Accelerated atherosclerosis is a major cause of death after 5 years in SLE.
Increased cardiovascular events due to traditional and non-traditional factors (e.g., antiphospholipid antibodies, low C3 levels).
Statin use reduces all-cause mortality in SLE patients with renal transplantation.
Increased risk of overall cancer and specific site-specific cancers (e.g., digestive, hematologic, lung, larynx, cervix, kidney, bladder, skin, thyroid) in SLE.
